We recommended to the Management the following measures:
a. 	Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and submit the complete RPCPPE for all types of property of the LGU;
b. 	Require the GSO to maintain the required Property Cards, respectively for all types of PPE owned by the LGU, and update and reconcile their property records with that of the Accounting Office’s Property Ledger Cards; and
c. 	Implement the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts procedures as provided under Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 to finally establish verifiable PPE balances as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive as the Chairperson of the Local School Board, to ensure compliance with the provisions of Section 6.1 of the JMC are properly carried out by the agency officials and employees concerned, and to instruct:
The LSB Secretary to ensure that copies of the SEF Budget are furnished to the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer are properly transmitted and duly stamped “RECEIVED”, signed and dated by the receiving officer of the office concerned;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit quarterly and annual reports using the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF utilization to the DepEd, copy furnished the local Sangguniang concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG; and
Designate personnel who will coordinate with the LGU website administrator to ensure that posting requirements of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s.2017 dated January 19, 2017, and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2024-021 dated February 15, 2024, were complied with.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the Local DRRM Officer to:
Refrain from using DRRMF for programs/projects/activities (PPAs) which are not directly related to pre-disaster preparedness, mitigation, response and rescue operations to ensure that it is utilized solely for approved DRRM-related PPAs;
Maximize DRRM projects and allocate sufficient resources towards disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, and response to significantly decrease the community’s vulnerability to disaster risks; and
Utilize the Fund strictly for disaster risk management activities in accordance with Section 3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the management assured compliance to the following:
a. 	The GAD Focal Person to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the responsibility center (RC) and RC Code for GFPS are properly established in the financial systems in compliance with COA Circular No. 2021-008;
b. 	The Municipal Accountant, in collaboration with the Municipal Budget Officer, establishes a distinct RC and RC code for the GFPS, ensuring that these are reflected in the computerized accounting system and budget systems; and
c. 	The Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the budget for GAD-related programs, activities, and expenses is properly aligned with the RC for GFPS.

We recommended to the management to take advantage on the use of ATM system in the payments of salaries, wages and other benefits instead of manual payments through cash advances to save man-hours and streamline the payment process.

10.12. 	We recommended and the management assured compliance to the following:
a. 	Direct the Petty Cash Fund Custodian to replenish the PCF upon reaching 75% utilization to maintain a sufficient cash balance for non-recurring petty expenses;
b. 	Established the PCF at the beginning of the year that is sufficient to pay for the non-recurring petty operating expenses of the agency for one month; and
c. 	Direct the PCF Custodian to strictly comply with the documentary requirements embodied under Section 1.2.2 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development with the Engineering     department to plan in carrying out accurate design and effective production of the projects to ensure immediate implementation when the funds are available;
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Engineer to expedite the implementation of these projects to provide the expected benefits to the intended beneficiaries on time and to act promptly on issues or constraints causing the delay of the implementation of the projects; and
Require the OIC-Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office once the projects are 100% complete for the proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive the following:
Reclassify the financial assistance recorded under the "Donation" account to the appropriate account as a "Due from National Government Agencies" in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, as outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-009. This will ensure that such transactions are properly accounted for and subject to liquidation, enabling better tracking of fund utilization;
Ensure that all financial assistance is properly documented and require the liquidation thereof as required under Section 4(6), 112 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009. This will facilitate proper monitoring and ensure that funds are utilized according to their intended purpose; and
Conduct follow-up and strict monitoring. The Municipal Accountant's Office should take immediate action to follow up with the schools to obtain the necessary liquidation documents. No further transfer of funds be made to those who failed to submit liquidation on the previously transferred funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) through the BAC Secretariat to submit the required supporting documents and comply with the contract review checklist to facilitate the review thereof. Moving forward, comply with COA Circular No. 2009-001 and pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
Refrain from designating the Internal Auditor as Petty Cash Custodian; and
Direct the Internal Audit Service to perform its mandated functions consistent with the Budget Circular No. 2008-005 and the Philippine Government Internal Audit Manual, to strengthen the Municipality’s Internal Control System, improve the existing systems and procedures, and promote transparency and accountability in the different levels of operations of the LGU.

a) For the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to:
i. direct the concerned department/personnel to ensure the full and
proper implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, draw the necessary accounting entries, and adhere to the
presentation and disclosure requirements, as provided in COA
Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
ii. assign additional personnel to the GSO to strengthen and enhance
their efficiency and capacity in meeting the growing demands of the
proper upkeep of the Municipality’s properties;
b) For the General Services Office to sharpen their controls and improve
procedures for managing the Municipality’s properties and equipment.
Among the physical controls that may be adopted by the Office are the
following:
i. conduct a proper physical count of properties at least annually and
prepare a comprehensive RPCPPE, ensuring that all relevant
information are accurately recorded and aligned with accounting
records;
ii. prepare and maintain necessary semi-expendable property reports,
ledgers, and monitoring, as outlined in COA Circular No. 2022-004
dated May 31, 2022;
iii. established a formalized inventory system with clearly defined
responsibilities for property custodians, and conduct regular
updates, ensuring that all required reports and ledgers are kept. This
will help ensure the accuracy of property and inventory records,
streamline asset tracking, reduce the risk of loss or mismanagement,
and ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of COA Circulars
and regulations.
c) For the Municipal Accounting Office to improve account reporting by
adhering to the guidelines of IPSAS 31 by disclosing the required data for
each class of Intangible Assets, especially the nature and amount of any
change in value that has a material effect in the current reporting period or is
expected to have a material effect in subsequent periods;
d) For the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to ensure that
the balances of the PPE accounts are reconciled on a regular basis, that any
discrepancies noted shall be immediately investigated and resolved, and that
appropriate adjustments are made to maintain the proper valuation of
accounts.
Furthermore, both Offices shall collaborate their efforts and practice regular
coordination to warrant a more accurate report of PPE balances.

remittance of taxes, contributions and premiums withheld on compensation as
outlined in the above-cited revenue regulations to avoid incurrence of penalties
and surcharges and denial of the delivery of general welfare program to the
general public.

to the SA providing details and terms for possible return of the remaining
funds to be used for other projects that can help improve the locality, or if the
said fund may be transferred to the General Fund as available for the LGU’s
disposition, otherwise the same be remitted to the SA to be cleared from the
books of accounts;
b) Consider appointing a focal person for each project or implementing office to
streamline the prompt implementation of PPAs;
c) Strengthen internal control mechanisms to improve project implementation
efficiency and ensure delivery of services to the recipient public while
adhering to the rules and regulations concerning fund transfers, including
liquidations; and
d) Communicate regularly with the SA in ensuring that funds are transferred on
time. The timeline of the transfer may be included in the Memorandum of
Agreement for easy reference and compliance.

We recommended that Management:
a) Develop calamity- and disaster-related activities that support the four
thematic areas of DRRM charged against unexpended balances from prior
periods and ensure to include these PPAs as a separate caption in the
LDRRMFIP;
b) Make certain that actual charges against the DRRM fund are in consonance
with the developed PPAs as included in the LDRRMFIP; and
c) Intensify follow-up mechanisms to NHA and other government bodies to
ensure that the housing units are fully implemented and properly turned over
to the beneficiaries as promptly as feasible.

ready, and in accordance with item 3.2 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated
November 4, 2020;

(MPMC), with representative/s from each concerned departments to be
spearheaded by the MPDC to discuss the physical status, as well as the
percentage of completion as to disbursements, allowing for responsive
decision-making to evaluate the feasibility of each PPA. If considered not
feasible, reprogram into other appropriate projects that better meet the
community’s evolving needs, in accordance with the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC
No. 1;
c) Prepare and submit a comprehensive Utilization Report for both current and
continuing appropriations to the Auditor’s Office on a regular basis for
periodic monitoring of planned PPAs; and
d) Consider specifying the implementation date in the AIP to guide the
management in preparing the necessary documents and procedures for the
implementation of development projects.

We recommended that Management:
a) Assign a focal person in the Municipal Accounting Office to monitor
adhering with the above-mentioned submission criteria, including supplying
copies of the SEF Accountability Forms on a regular basis with transmittal
reports duly receipted by the DepED Makilala District Office;
b) Include the Local Sanggunian in the quarterly and annual dissemination of
reports;
c) Ensure posting the SEF Utilization Reports in at least three (3) conspicuous
public places for transparency and accountability; and
d) Revisit the programs financed by the Continuing Appropriations, assess
their necessity or applicability, and consider reprogramming into other
relevant projects that better meet the learners’ developing requirements.

a) Prioritize funding for SWM activities, thereby utilizing the 20%
Development Fund and Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund, as
SWM programs intersect with both areas. Include the precise PPAs in the
AIP with a set schedule to ensure that the program objectives are reached
for all participants;
b) Add personnel to the MENRO to enhance their capacity in enforcing SWM
programs, monitor and assess their implementation. This will also
strengthen record-keeping practices to ensure timely data analysis and
improvement; and
c) Implement public involvement activities through the LCE’s office and
Sangguniang Bayan, which are essential components of the MGM’s
sustainable environmental management strategy. Information
Technology (IT) platforms can be used to carry out actions that advance
public goals, raise public awareness, and encourage participation in
community development projects.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)
through its Secretariat, to submit the original copies of awarded contracts and
related supporting documents to the audit team, duly tabbed for ease of
reference, within the time frame specified by the regulations, so that evaluation
results are notified to the management in a timely manner, and corrective
actions, if any, are carried out effectively.

We recommended that Management prioritize hiring personnel who will be
detailed in the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat Office to assist in
fulfilling administrative functions, such as preparing and submitting
Procurement Monitoring Reports and APPs within the time frame specified by
the aforementioned regulation, and providing a copy to the Office of the City
Auditor to ensure proper notice.

Development Office (MPDO), appoint personnel to coordinate with various
offices in the preparation and timely submission of the required reports for
uploading in the FDP portal, to ensure compliance with the guidelines and raise
people's awareness of the available public funds and the amount allocated for
development projects in their local communities.

or update of project outcomes for all projects; and

complete supporting documents for timely review and validation of
accomplishments.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:

a) For the General Services Office to strengthen their controls and improve
procedures in the management of the Municipality’s properties and
equipment. Among the physical controls that may be adopted by the Office
are the following:

i. Conduct of complete and proper physical count of properties at least
annually;

ii. Maintenance of required reports and up-to-date records to monitor
the existence, condition, and movement of PPEs at any given time;

iii. Render the required reports for Road Networks, maintain a Local
Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road
Network Property Card for all roads and its components, with the
support of the Municipal Engineering Office;

iv. Create a systematic and periodic disposal plan for unserviceable
assets in coordination with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee;
and,

v. Periodic review of insurance coverage of all insurable assets to
ensure that the Municipality’s properties are adequately protected
against any damage to or loss due to fire, earthquake, storm, or other
fortuitous events/casualty in accordance with COA Circular No. 2018-
002 dated May 31, 2018.

b) For the Municipal Accounting Office to enhance practices in the reporting of
accounts by:

i. Making the necessary adjusting entries to recognize the appropriate
accumulated depreciation amount and come up with the correct value
of the PPE accounts;

ii. Revisiting the IIRUP for CY 2021 onwards, and creating the necessary
journal entries to drop from the books all PPE items which were
already disposed of and demolished;

iii. Adhering to the guidelines of IPSAS 31 by disclosing the required
data for each class of Intangible Assets, especially the nature and
amount of any change in value that has a material effect in the current
reporting period or is expected to have a material effect in subsequent
periods; and,

iv. Disclosing the total Road Network System in the Notes to Financial
Statements to provide the end users with adequate information
regarding the accounts following the provisions of COA Circular No.
2015-008.

c) For the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to ensure that
the balances of the PPE accounts are reconciled on a regular basis and that
discrepancies noted shall be immediately investigated and resolved to
maintain the proper valuation of accounts.

Furthermore, both Offices shall collaborate their efforts and practice regular
coordination to warrant a more accurate report of PPE balances.

d) To assign additional personnel to the GSO to strengthen and enhance their
efficiency and capacity in addressing the growing demands of the proper
upkeep of the Municipality’s properties and assets.

We recommended that Management:

a) Come up with detailed inventory control mechanisms that will capture the
proper receipt, storage, issuance, monitoring/controlling and recording of
inventory items;

b) Further support the GSO by assigning additional personnel to assist in
adhering to the proper protocols for the effective management of inventory
items;

c) Through the GSO –

i. Maintain up-to-date stock cards for each type of supplies to account
for their receipt and disposition;

ii. Consolidate all RIS monthly into the RSMI and submit them to the
MAO for JEV preparation. This is to ensure that consumed supplies
are promptly accounted for within the applicable period;

d) Through MAO and GSO to regularly reconcile the balances per ledger cards
and stock cards; and

e) Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories,
with the assistance of all offices, and to prepare the RPCI for submission to
the Municipal Accountant for necessary adjustments and then to the
Auditor’s Office for review not later than July 31 and January 31 of each
year.

We recommended that Management:

a) Regularly communicate and request documents such as amortization
schedules with banks and other lending institutions, to facilitate the
amortization of the loans availed by the LGU and the proper identification of
the current and non-current portion of the loans and other liability accounts;

b) Examine other liability accounts related to the aforementioned observation
and identify the settlement of such accounts, whether within one year or
more, to facilitate the proper classification and disclosure of the results of
its evaluation, if any, in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management:

a) Assess whether the PPAs originally funded are feasible to be implemented,
otherwise may consider reprogramming to more suitable projects, programs
and activities (PPAs) in consonance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-
1, and other pertinent guidelines to encompass the four (4) thematic areas
of DRRM such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness,
disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery. Integrate them
into the AIP for approval of the local Sanggunian to serve as a roadmap in
DRRMF utilization;

b) Include the PPAs charged against the unexpended balances from prior
periods, as a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP;

c) Monitor the unexpended balances to be recognized under the Trust
Liabilities-DRRM account including its utilization and balances. Ensure the
accounting and reporting guidelines are adhered to, based on COA Circular
2012-002 and RA 10121 known as the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Act of 2010;

d) Through the MAO, draw journal entries on the unrecorded PPE items in the
General Fund books and be recognized with depreciation charges. Moving
forward, ensure that PPE accounts are properly recorded in the TF books
and transfer them in the GF once completed, issued to the end-user and
ownership has been transferred in adherence to COA Circular 2012-002;

e) Streamline procurement activities to improve the readiness of the LGU in
terms of disasters and capacitate them to respond in emergencies. Intensify
follow-up mechanisms to NHA and other government bodies to fully
implement the housing units and properly turn them over to the
beneficiaries; and

f) DRRMO and the related departments coordinate closely to meet program
requirements and financial information be prepared timely for relevant
decision-making activities.

We recommended that Management:

a) Coordinate with the concerned departments to ensure effective planning,
implementation and reporting of PPAs and evaluate the feasibility of each
PPA on a regular basis. If considered not feasible, reprogram into other
appropriate projects that better address the evolving needs of the
community;

b) Evaluate the sufficiency of the support staff in MPDO to strictly monitor the
implementation of PPAs. Consider creating a specific committee/team under
the office that will spearhead the planning to ensure the proper evaluation of
PPA implementation and reporting thereafter;

c) Prepare and submit the Utilization Report for both current and continuing
appropriations to the Auditor’s Office on a regular basis for periodic
monitoring of planned PPAs; and

d) Consider specifying the implementation date in the AIP to guide the
management when to prepare the necessary documents and the actions to
be needed in the implementation.

We recommended that Management:

a) Evaluate the necessary requirements of the PPAs not implemented and
incomplete whether they are feasible and if not, may submit status reports
to the SA providing details and terms for possible return of the remaining
funds to be used for other projects that can help improve the locality or if
the said fund may be transferred to the General Fund as available for the
LGU’s disposition, otherwise the same be remitted to the SA to be cleared
from the books of accounts;

b) Consider appointing a focal person for each project or implementing office to
streamline the prompt implementation of PPAs; and

c) Strengthen internal control mechanisms to improve the efficiency in
implementing the projects and to ensure compliance with the rules and
regulations concerning fund transfers, including liquidations.

We recommended that Management:

a) Set the GAD Agenda or identify priority gender issues, GAD mandates or
targets to be addressed over a one-year or three-year term of the LCE and
ensure the provision of adequate resources;

b) Conduct gender analysis using the gender-related information existing in
the GAD database to identify and understand gaps and issues between
genders in order to develop a more gender-sensitive PPAs. Tap assistance
from other institutions that could provide gender-related information such
as but not limited to Philippine Statistics Authority, academe, CSOs, NGAs
like Department of Agriculture (DA), Department of Environment and Natural
Resources (DENR), Department of Agrarian Reform (DAR), Department of
Labor and Employment (DOLE), Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) and
others;

c) May consider collaborating with local stakeholders to better formulate PPAs
to be included in the GPB, and proposal of training programs to toughen
GFPS in GAD-related concerns and outcomes;

d) Revisit the Philippine Plan for Gender-Responsive Development 1995-2025
which cites programs under different sectors like human development,
economic and industrial, infrastructure and technology, and special
concerns sector that will help in achieving Sustainable Development Goals
or Agenda 2030 set by the United Nations General Assembly in 2015; and

e) Establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team within the Municipality to
monitor and evaluate the implementation of the GAD PPAs and to ensure
the efficiency in managing its resources and effective observance of its
objectives. Assessment of program objectives should be reported through
the preparation of GAD Evaluation Report.

We recommended that Management:

a) Formally establish the Internal Audit Service / Unit with SG-24, the head of
a municipal government department. Internal auditors shall be detached
from ordinary or routine operating responsibilities, as indicated in
Administrative Order No. 278 issued by then-President Corazon C. Aquino
in 1992, to fully operate in accordance with its statutory functions;

b) Evaluate the existing manpower complement through the Municipal Human
Resource Management Office (MHRMO) to determine possible personnel
transfers to form the IAS/IAU, change staffing patterns, or create new
internal auditor roles in compliance with CSC Memorandum Circular No. 19
s. 1992, and other pertinent issuances; and

c) Prioritize the disposition of issues and concerns raised by the previous
acting IAS to improve government operations and service delivery.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a.) 	Direct the Petty Cash Fund Custodian to replenish the PCF upon reaching 75% utilization so that sufficient cash balance is available to cover expenses of small payments of the agency;
b.) 	Require the PCF Custodian and the Municipal Accountant to observe the proper replenishment of the PCF and use the forms prescribed such as Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs) supported with summary/list of PCVs; and
c.) 	Direct the Municipal Accountant and PCF Custodian to comply with the documentary requirements embodied under Section 1.2.2 of COA Circular 2012-001.

Recommendations:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a.) 	Create an Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory count over the LGU’s Local Road Network. A physical count of Local Road Network should be done at least once a year and prepare Report on the Physical Count of Road Network System and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor;
b.) 	Direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant, General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
c.) 	Direct the General Services Officer-Designate to maintain the Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and to keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; and
d.) 	Require the Municipal Accountant the following reporting procedures:
Prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card, respectively. Support the JV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads, working paper on the distribution of costs for the road components, and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost for road components with no available cost per registry.
Keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and
Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat and the Municipal Engineer to comply with the regulations and furnish the Audit Team with the required copy of contracts along with their supporting documents within five (5) days after the perfection thereof for the timely review, evaluation and inspection of the same. For those beyond the threshold of the Audit Team, after auditorial and legal review, it will be forwarded to the Technical Audit Sector at the COA, Regional Office X.

Recommendations:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a.) Instruct the Municipal Accountant and GSO-Designate to comply with the provisions of Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs specifically on the recording of supplies and materials as Inventories.  All purchases of supplies and materials should be taken up as inventories except those purchases from Petty Cash Fund;
b.) Require the Municipal Accountant that Supplies Ledger Cards be kept updated; and the GSO-Designate to maintain Stock Cards for each inventory item received which should be reconciled with the SLC in the Accounting department; and
c.) Require the GSO-Designate to prepare and submit the Report on the Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) monthly to the accounting department for the recording of the utilization in the Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the inventories accounts.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the proposed correcting journal entries.
Moreover, conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to eliminate the difference.

Recommendation:
We recommended for the Management to refrain the practice of charging prior years’ expenses against the current year’s appropriation. This shall be initiated by incurring expenses according only to the available appropriation. If the incurrence of expenses during year-end cannot be dispensed with in the exigency of service, unpaid obligations shall be recorded as payables to validate their payments in the succeeding years. For the process to be valid however, said payables shall be duly supported with complete documentation.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009, primarily on its Chart of Accounts for the appropriate use of the account in the recording of transactions.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive that coordination should be made between the Human Resource Management Officer and the Municipal Accountant in the granting of loans to its employees and strictly implement the minimum net take home pay of P5,000 every month.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the General Services Officer-Designate should thoroughly accomplish data on the purchase orders to provide the necessary information and basis in determining the reliability of the procurement process. Likewise, copies of purchase orders should be furnished to the auditor, including the related supporting papers/attachments, within five (5) days after approval.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to require business establishments to provide official receipts, sales invoice (cash or charge), and commercial receipts/invoices as evidence of receipt of payment upon the release of check to suppliers;
Require the Municipal Accountant to check completeness of the disbursement vouchers upon submission to the Audit Team for post-audit; and
Comply the submission of receipts as shown in Appendix P and Q to minimize issuance of Notice of Suspension.

Recommendations:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a.) 	Require the General Service Officer-Designate to strictly comply with the preparation of required reports enumerated in Chapter IV of the Handbook on Property and Supply Management and Section 4.6 of PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
b.) Direct the Municipal Accountant that complete supporting documents should be attached to the disbursement vouchers before payment shall be made.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management stop the practice of donating government funds to religious activities and private organizations pursuant Section 335 of Republic Act No. 7160 so as to avoid unnecessary depletion of government funds.

observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018 | During the occurrence of typhoon Vinta, the office of the MSWD under Ms. Zenaida O. Alcoran, MSWDO at that time, was the one who identified the victims who volunteered to be paid for cash for work. Our office only received and processed payrolls for CFW and saw to it that the supporting papers were sufficient enough so that the needed funds and supplies of welfare goods can be released immediately to the victims and workers. As to the implementation and utilization of the Recovery and Rehabilitation Plan, the MSWDO and LDRRMO are the officers responsible for it.

OIC MSWDO and LDRRMO Mr. Nestor Patalinghug explained that after the occurrence of typhoon Vinta, the people- victims of flood in the business center of Maranding exerted efforts voluntarily to clean up sticky mud that came inside their houses and establishment, so when the assigned personnel from MSWDO/LDRRMO came to assess the damage, the mud were already cleaned up. observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the management to observe the proper utilization of the LDRRM funds pursuant to DBM – DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013 and COA issued Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 27, 2010; March 25, 2013 and September 12, 2012, respectively. We recommend that the management:
observations.managementAction:
In a letter response from the Office of the Municipality Mayor of Lala to Ms. Marilou C. Mampao, Supervising Auditor thru Ms. Macrina M. Gentiles, Audit Team Leader dated January 19, 2017 re: The use of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) was not in accordance with Section 21 of R.A. 10121, NDRRMC-DBM-DILF Joint Memorandum Circular No. 13-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-02, the Municipal Mayor justified and we quote:
"That under joint memorandum circular No. 2013-1 of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG there appears a provision, citing item 6.0 on Policies and Procedures on the utilization of LDRRMF, specifically sub items 6.4 and 6.5.
Evidently, any unused amount of the LDRRMF at the end of the year shall accrue as a special trust fund and the same maybe released or utilized exclusively for the PROGRAMS/PROJECTS, and ACTIVITIES (PPA) to support disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRM Council within the next five (5) years. 
Further, while balances at the end of the year are held as Special Trust Fund, the same maybe released and utilized for new expenditure items upon submission of a revised AIP as approved by the local sangguniang and Local Chief Executive (LCE) upon recommendation of the LDRRMC in accordance with the approved LDRRMP to which the Municipal Government of Lala had appropriately complied with.
Now therefore, the amount utilized (Php 6,131,482) from our Special Trust Fund as an additional funds for the construction of Phase II of the Centralized Evacuation Center is in accordance with the existing rules cited above. All of the municipal government’s unexpended/unused LDRRMF AIP balances on 30% AND 70% in the previous years from 2011 to 2015 had been already transferred to Special Trust Fund. LDRRMF utilization report dated December 31, 2015 signed by the Municipal Accountant is attached herein as an integral part of this letter.
It is noteworthy, and by reason of transparency, prior to the utilization for the release of the said special trust fund, the Municipal Government of Lala through the undersigned had referred the matter to the DBM Regional Office 10 through RD Annabelle M. Atillo. Even after the issued AOM, the municipal government had communicated to the latter office for distinct and definite answer. During the Regional Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Summit on the 1st quarter in Cagayan de Oro City facilitated by the Office of the Civil Defense and participated by Mayors and LDRRMO’s in whole Region 10 the same response was reiterated by DBM 10.
Hence, with all honesty, and with all our supporting documents and a confirmation from the respected authority whom we sought assistance and whom we believe is the rightful agency on the issue at hand, we humbly claim that the municipal government did not incur any lapses to the above-mentioned laws as alleged by your good office. We solely based our arguments on the said joint memo circular and by the oral and written answer of the DBM to which the undersigned believed that the same was referred by the regional office to the national office in Manila."

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the management to:

Direct the members of the GFPS, thru the MSWDO, to spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the GAD Database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation. They shall conduct examination of the agency’s level of gender mainstreaming or extent of the gender responsiveness of the municipal programs and activities, the level of gender awareness and competence of its personnel and the presence or absence of enabling mechanisms that support gender mainstreaming.

observations.managementAction: In response to this observation, the LGU GAD Focal Point System headed by the MSWDO commits the set up and maintenance of a GAD Database for the Municipality of Lala. With regards to the assistance given to Senior Citizen and PWD activities, the management explained that they will stop the payments of cash assistance out of the GAD Budget, instead allocate an amount on their Local Budget for Senior Citizen and PWD. observations.recommendation:

observations.managementAction: On the issue regarding the charges to SEF, the management explained that it has been practiced in their Municipality to charge such expenditures to Special Education Fund and they were not aware that the provisions stated prohibits the charging of such expenditures. observations.recommendation: Establish and adopt the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) payroll system account the soonest possible time in conformity with the DBM National Payroll System to attain and ensure greater efficiency, economy, transparency, and accountability in the government’s human resource processes.
observations.managementAction: The management still continued to pay salaries of the officials and employees   thru the cashier and not adopt the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) payroll system. However, during the exit conference with the Municipal Mayor, he confirmed that the LGU LALA will comply the said recommendation as soon as the LBP will operate near in their locality.

observations.recommendation:
For Dormant unliquidated cash advances:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare an aging of unliquidated cash advances to support the request for write-off, indicate in the remarks column the existence of the applicable conditions such as:
unknown whereabouts of the accountable officer/employee/debtor;
that he/she could not be located despite diligent efforts to find him/her;
exhaustion of all possible remedies by the Management to collect the receivables;
To demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers.
Require the Local Chief Executive to file for request of authority to write-off on unliquidated cash advances as mentioned above to COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/ or Supervising Auditor (SA).
For Unliquidated Cash Advances on active service:
The Municipal Accountant should ensure that all cash advances are liquidated immediately as soon as the purpose has been served, otherwise send letter of demand.
Refrain from processing disbursement vouchers for a new cash advance for those with previous unsettled cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant should effect the withholding of salary to concerned officials.
Ensure cash advance are liquidated after its purpose has been served. For failure of the accountable officer to settle/liquidate outstanding accountabilities as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served, send demand letters enjoining the officials and employees concerned.

We have recommended the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer should discontinue the practice of granting cash advances without the approved payroll or estimated detailed list/estimates of expenses to support the amount drawn in compliance to COA Circular 97-002 to avoid excessive cash advances.
